name: Food that Climbed the Food Chain and Controlled it
description:
We are talking about humans!

Two million years ago, the early humans jumped the food chain to become the biggest predators of the land. We hunted animals in large numbers driving the biggest mammals on the planet to extinction. But, 12000 years ago, something changed. We exited from the food chain and started growing our food, creating an all new food chain for ourselves. 
From being food ourselves to completely controlling our food ecosystem, we have come a long way! Ever wondered what our journey must have been like to become the versions we are today? 

Join Harsha Jagadeesh, as she explores our journey as species and everything we created and destroyed to make today happen.

name: Whose Waste Is It Anyway?
description:
When we hear the word “waste”, we often think of the things we throw away. But waste is much more than a bin full of discarded objects. It can be food that never gets eaten, resources used carelessly, effort that is overlooked, pollutants that enter our bodies, or materials that one system rejects and another depends on.

This walkthrough invites us to look at waste not as an endpoint, but as part of a larger cycle. As we move through the exhibition, we will ask where waste comes from and who lives with its consequences. 

Join our Experimentor, Sai, as we follow the many lives of waste. By the end, we may find that waste tells us as much about what we value as it does about what we discard.

name: The Rat Race! Entangled Appetites in CALORIE
description: Pests, harbingers of plague, indicators of famines - Rodents, often thought to be at the bottom of the food chain, are ever present entities in our food systems. 'The Rat Race! Entangled Appetites in CALORIE' explores the various roles of rodents in CALORIE. Join Aashka, Megha, Tejashree and Vinaya to discuss the lives and deeds of our small friends across farms, factories, cities and plates.

name: Crafting Comics
description:
What kind of stories can you tell through comics?

Join Orijit Sen as he explores how to create stories, characters and worlds through the medium of comics. Wander through his exhibit “Mapping Mapusa Market” searching for inspiration in its many layered stories and characters. Learn to create your own comic and bring your stories to life through visual storytelling.

This workshop is open to everyone and does not require prior knowledge or skill with drawing.

name: Mapping a Market in Motion
description:
How do you capture the past, present and possible futures of a market town? 

Join artist Orijit Sen for a deep dive into his exhibit ‘Mapping Mapusa Market.’ The exhibit captures the energy of Goa’s historic market—a bustling hub where homemade goods like pickles, poe, and chorizo mingle with imported products. More than a tourist destination, Mapusa is a living, fragile organism shaped by competition, trade, and change—both local and global.

Drawing on the Mapusa Mogi project, in this talk Orijit delves into the social, cultural and economic functions that the market performs for the people of Goa. These explorations involve a mix of visual documentation, socio-historical mapping and graphic narration. It takes forward his long and continuing association with Mapusa as an artist, teacher and documentarian.

name: We Consume Therefore We Are
description:
Cogito, ergo sum - "I think, therefore I am."
Descartes' famous adage proposes the existence of the self through an act of the mind. 

But is the mind really all there is to who we are? What if we move beyond the cogito and into the gut? 

‘We Consume Therefore We Are’ attempts to describe the CALORIE exhibition-season through the lens of philosophy, exploring what it means to consume; and how ethical, cultural and political factors shape food as central to our existence.

What does it mean to taste something? 
What makes an object ‘food’?
Are we what we eat?

Join our Experimentor, Aashka on a journey from the field to the plate; exploring the ethics, aesthetics and epistemology of food!

name: Art, Science or Something Else?
description:
As Science Gallery Bengaluru and Shared Ecologies launch the second iteration of its collaborative grant programme, Entangled Ecosystems, artist Ravi Agarwal and historian of science Jahnavi Phalkey come together to reflect on the possibilities and challenges of working across art and science.

Looking back at the outcomes of the first grant, the conversation will explore questions of knowledge, collaboration, audience, and public engagement. What happens when different ways of knowing and making come together? When do collaborations succeed, and what can be learned when they do not?

Join us for an evening of reflection and discussion as we look ahead to the next chapter of the programme.

name: The Elephant In the Room
description:
The world is not only ours. Scientists estimate that there are nearly 8.7 million eukaryotic species on this planet, of which only 2.1 million have been discovered so far; and humans are just one of them!

Whether it's livestock on the farm or microbes in the pickle jar, non-human life forms are not only present everywhere but are an integral part of our food systems. However, the industrialization of food production has indavertently affected the lives and behaviours of these organisms.

Join our Experimentors Thejaswini and Aashka in a walkthrough of the CALORIE exhibition season, as they address the 'elephant in the room' by re-examining food systems as the dynamic ecological landscapes that they are. Learn about edible insects, endangered fish species, native varieties of rice, and much more!

name: Milk Teeth: Children, Childhood, Care and CALORIE
description:
While childhood often feels like a natural and universal stage of life, ideas about children and childhood have varied dramatically across time and place.

From factories of the Industrial Revolution to Mid-Day Meals in the schools of today, this walkthrough traces how childhood has been imagined historically and how it continues to be transformed. Engaging with themes of labour, memory, technology, and public health, we will explore the relationships between growing bodies, social systems, and the futures we imagine for children.

Join our Experimentor Ayati at CALORIE, to examine the systems that nurture childhood and children's access to nutrition.

name: Eco-lie: The Truth About Sewage Superheroes
description:
What if the city's most essential workers are invisible to the naked eye?

"Eco-lie" challenges our anthropocentric understanding of urban infrastructure by foregrounding E. coli, the bacterium typically villainised as a pathogen, as a vital laborer in water systems. This interactive workshop uses visual storytelling, reflective exercises, and participatory discussion to explore how bacterial communities help treat sewage, upcycle nutrients, and sustain the wetlands that nourish urban agro-ecologies (both plant and fish farms). Through microscopic imagery, ecological diagrams, and oral testimonies from the East Kolkata Wetlands, participants will reimagine sewage not as waste but as a site of transformation. This workshop invites you to develop more-than-human empathy and recognize the political dimensions of E. coli's existence in the Anthropocene.

name: A Sketchbook Tour Inside the City's Gut
description: Join artist and urban researcher Pratyasha Nath as she takes you inside the creative journey of "The Swamp That Feeds the City," a mixed-media comic installation on the East Kolkata Wetlands at CALORIE. From first encounters with sewage canals to conversations with farmers and fishers whose livelihoods depend on the city's flush, complex ecological research was transformed into visual storytelling. This talk traces the artist's path through field visits, archival research, and experimental art-making—revealing how a 3,000-hectare wetland functions as Kolkata's digestive system, processing 550,000 cubic metres of sewage daily while producing fish and vegetables that return to the city's tables. Through sketches, failed experiments, and finished works, discover how art can make visible the invisible metabolic flows that keep cities alive.
